etymology: From Old English frod (wise by experience). Fróði is also a legendary Danish king famed for peace.
lore: Bearer of the One Ring. His Icelandic name carries mythic weight — King Fróði ruled during a Golden Age, making the name bittersweet.

etymology: Old English samwis (half-wise). Icelandic Samvís mirrors this: sam- (half) + vís (wise). Tolkien chose it to suggest humility.
lore: Frodo's loyal gardener. Despite the self-deprecating name, Tolkien called Sam the chief hero of the tale.

etymology: Short for Meriadoc. Icelandic Glöðr captures the nickname's spirit from glaðr (glad, merry) — tone over etymology.
lore: Meriadoc Brandybuck of Buckland, esquire of Rohan. Glöðr suits his bright, quick-witted character.

etymology: Short for Peregrin, Latin peregrinus (wanderer). Peppi is a phonetic Icelandicisation preserving the playful sound.
lore: Peregrin Took, youngest of the Fellowship. He became a Knight of Gondor and later Thain of the Shire.

etymology: Old Norse gandr (magical staff) + álfr (elf). The name appears in the Völuspá — Tolkien took it directly from the Eddas.
lore: The name predates Tolkien by centuries, making Gandálfr the most authentic form. His true Quenya name is Olórin.

etymology: Sindarin: ara- (kingly) + gorn (revered). Retained unchanged; some editions use Arahorn to ease Icelandic pronunciation.
lore: Heir of Isildur, rightful King of Gondor. His Sindarin name directly declares his destiny.

etymology: Sindarin: laeg (keen, fresh) + golas (foliage). Retained as a proper name; some gloss it Lauflindur (leaf-slender).
lore: Prince of the Woodland Realm of Mirkwood. Renowned for his elvish sight and skill with a bow.

etymology: Old Norse Gimlé — the heavenly hall surviving Ragnarök in the Völuspá. Tolkien borrowed it from the Eddas; kept unchanged.
lore: Son of Glóin, sole dwarf in the Fellowship. He famously befriended the elf Legolas.

etymology: Sindarin: bor (enduring) + mir (jewel). A fitting name for a man of great honour. Retained unchanged in Icelandic.
lore: Son of Denethor. His fall to the Ring's temptation and sacrifice at Amon Hen is one of the tale's most tragic moments.

etymology: Old English searu-mann (man of craft). Could be Listmaður in Icelandic, but the Old English form is kept. Quenya: Curunír.
lore: Head of the Order of Wizards, fallen to his desire for the Ring. His name betrays his nature as a craftsman corrupted by cleverness.

etymology: Quenya saura (vile, putrid). Sáron adapts the long vowel to Icelandic orthography. His name before his fall was Mairon (the Admirable).
lore: The Dark Lord of Mordor. He forged the One Ring to control all other Ring-bearers.

etymology: Anglicisation of the horrible swallowing sound he makes. His original name Sméagol (Sméagull) is from OE smēagan (to creep into).
lore: Once a hobbit-like creature corrupted by the One Ring over five centuries. His dual nature is one of Tolkien's greatest creations.

etymology: Old English: éoh (warhorse) + wyn (joy). Kept in Icelandic as OE and ON are closely related, making it natural for an Icelandic reader.
lore: Shield-maiden of Rohan who slew the Witch-king. Her name's equine meaning fits Rohan's horse-culture perfectly.

etymology: Old English þēoden (king, lord of a people). Icelandic Þjóðann uses the native þ (thorn), from þjóð (people) — directly cognate.
lore: King of Rohan. His name simply means king — fitting for a noble ruler who died heroically at Pelennor Fields.

etymology: Sindarin: ar- (noble) + wen (maiden). Kept unchanged. Undómiel means Evenstar (Kveldsstjarna in Icelandic).
lore: Daughter of Elrond, called the Evenstar. She chose mortality for love of Aragorn, mirroring the tale of Lúthien and Beren.

etymology: Sindarin: el (star) + rond (vaulted hall). Retained unchanged. Rivendell in Icelandic is Klofningur or Rifdalur.
lore: Half-elven lord who chose immortality. He bore Vilya, mightiest of the three Elvish Rings.

etymology: Sindarin: galad (radiance) + riel (garlanded maiden). Quenya: Altáriel; Telerin: Alatáriel. Kept unchanged.
lore: Lady of Lothlórien, bearer of Nenya the Ring of Water. She refused the One Ring when offered it by Frodo.

etymology: Tolkien coined this to sound hobbit-like. Icelandic Bilbó adds an accent to signal the long vowel per Icelandic phonology.
lore: Bilbo Baggins of Bag End, protagonist of The Hobbit. His adventure set in motion the events of the War of the Ring.

etymology: Aragorn's Ranger name. Gandrekkur: ganga (to stride) + rekkr (warrior). A fine calque preserving both meaning and heroic register.
lore: Name given to Aragorn by the Bree-folk. Gandrekkur feels natural in Icelandic — the kind of name a taciturn Ranger might earn.

etymology: She + Old English lob (spider). Icelandic Kóngulóin is the Icelandic word for spider given a definite feminine form.
lore: The last child of Ungoliant, a giant spider in the pass of Cirith Ungol. Defeated by Sam wielding the Phial of Galadriel.
